California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 
■8′  ■990
Honorable Phillip D. w)aran
Assemblynan, Thirty-Fourth District
State capitol
Sacramento, CA 9 58l-4
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. I-9 0-4 59
Dear Assembl)rman w)rman:
This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
of telephone advice provided to a mernber of your staff on July 10,
1990, regarding the use of carnpaign funds under the provisions of
the Poliaical Reform Act (the trActtr).r we do not have sufficient
facts to provide a definite anse/er to your guestions; therefore,
we consider your letter to be a request for informal assistance
pursuant to iegulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed) .2
OUESTIONS
1. Itay an elected official- use campaign funds to pay for
expenses incurred to attend a convention?
2. If the elected official 
receives a scholarship to cover
the cost of attending the convention, may he use the scholarship
funds to reimburse his canpaign account?
CONCLUSTONS
1. An elected official 
may use campaign funds to pay for
expenses associated with his attendance at a convention if the
evint is reasonablv related to a political, 
legislative, 
or
governmental purpose. However, if the expenditure results in a
1' 
covernment Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the covernnent Code unless otherwi-se indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18OOo, et seq. All- references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2  1nfoこ■lal assistance does not
immunity provided by an oPinion
tion 83■■4, Regu■ation ■8329(c)
provide the requestor with the
or formal written advice. (Sec-
)
428 J Street, Suite 8O0 . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804´ 0807 0 (916)322‐ 5660


File No. 9o-4 59
Page 2
substantial personal benefit to the candidate, the expenditure
must be directlv related to a political, 
legislative, or
governnental purpose.
2. If the elected official 
receives a scholarship to cover
the cost of attending the convention, he nay deposit the scholar-
ship funds in his campaign bank account.
FACTS
You $/ish to attend a convention and pay for all exPenses out
of your canpaign funds. At a later date, you intend to apply for
a scholarship to cover these expenses. Assuning that you are
granted a scholarship, you wish to deposit any funds received into
your carnpaiqn bank account.
ANALYSI S
The Act regulates the use of campai.gn funds and requires
that all contributions deposited into the campaign bank account be
held in trust for expenses associated wi.th the election of the
candidate to a speciiic office or exPenses associated with holding
that office. 
(Section a52o2. ) In general, expenditures of
campaign funds nust be reasonablv related to a political,
goverm,oental, or leqisLative purpose. (section 85801.) However,
in expenditure which results in a substantial personal benefit to
the clndidate [ust be directlv retated to a poJ.itical, legisla-
tive, or governnental purpose. (Section 8580L.) A substantial
personal benefit to the clndidate is one which results in a direct
lersonal benefit of Dore than $1oo to the candidate or elected
officer. 
(Section 85800(b) (2).) 
An expenditure of campaign funds
results in a direct personal benefit when, within six months of
the expenditure of canPaign funds, the candidate:
(1) Realizes an increase in his or her incorne
or assets, or a decrease in his or her expenses or
liabilities, 
of more than $100 from the
expenditure; or
(2) Actually makes personal use of the asset
obtained as a result of the expend j-ture.
Regulation 18580(a) (1) and (2),
copy enclosed.
Although you have advised us that you intend to attend a
convention, you have not informed us $rhether this event is related
to a political, 
legislative, or governmental purpose. Because we
lack lnfornation about the type of event you will be attending, we
cannot deternine srhether an expenditure of carnpaign funds to at-
tend this event is permissible under the Act.
After you attend the convention and pay for all expenses, you
intend to apply for a scholarship to cover your expenses. These


Fi-Ie No. 90-459
Page 3
funds will be either a gift, 
income or a contribution. 
we lack
sufficient informati,on about the convention you will attend and
have no knowtedge of the criteria to be used for awardinq scholar-
ships. Tfrerefore, we cannot deternine how the scholarship funds
should be classified. 
To assist you in making this determination
rre are providing you with the following discussion which clarifies
the terDs rrgif t, 't 
n incor[e'r and rrcontributionrr f or Purposes of the
Act.
The Act inposes certaj,n filing 
and disclosure obligations on
public officials. 
(Section a72oo. ) Sources of income of $25o or
more and gifts of $50 or more must be disclosed in your yearly
statement of economic interests. 
(Section a72o7. ) Contributions
are subject to separate recordkeeping and reporting requirenents
(sections 84200-84226), and are subject to contribution limits.
(Sections 85100-85400. )
The prinary distinction between a gift (Section 82028) and
incone (SLction 82030) is whether the recipient has provided equal
consideration in exchange for the PaylTrent. where a payment is
received and the recipient does not provide consideration of equal
or greater value to the source of the pa)rment, the payrnent is
coniidered a gift. 
conversely, where the recipient can
demonstrate that he or she has provided equa)' or greater
consideration for the paynent, the pa)rnent is treated as incone'
(Torrance Advice Letter, No. A-89-648, coPy encl-osed. )
Equal consideration means that each party receives benefits
of equal value. (In re Burciaqa (L975) 2 FPPC Ops. 17, copy
enclosed.) we do not know the source of the scholarship, nor do
we know whether you will be providing services to the donor.
Ho$rever, it would appear that you, as the recipient of the
scholarship, will have received a payment for which you have not
provided consideration. The pa)rment wouLd thus be a gift to you
ihich would be reportable in your statement of economic interests.
AdditionalIy, the Comrnission has enacted Regulation L8228
$rhich states in pertinent part as follows:
(a) Except as provided in Sections LA726.A,
18727, L8727.5 and 18728, payments made to an
elected officer or candidate for his or her food
are qifts.
(b) Except as provided in section L8727.5,
payments made to an elected officer or candidate
for his or her accommodations or transportation are
gifts unless the accommodations or transportation
provided to the elected officer or candidate are
directly in connection with canpaign activities,
including attendance at political 
fundraisers.
Regiulation 18228, copy enclosed.


File No. 9 0-4 59
Page 4
we are enclosing copies of pertinent regulations and suggest
that you review them for purposes of deternining whether the
scholirship funds are income or gifts to you.
Alternatively, the scholarship funds rnay be a contribution'
section 82015 Provides that:
ttcontributionrr means a paynent, a forgiveness
of a loan, a paynent of a loan by a third party, or
an enforceabre promise to make a payment except to
the extent that fuI1 and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not nade for political
purposes .
Additionally, the conmission has determined that a pa)ment is nade
for political 
PurPoses if it is:
(2) Received by or made at the behest of:
(A) A candidate, unless it is clear frorn sur-
rounding circumstances that the Payment r{'as
received or made at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
Itatus as an office holder. The term rtpaym€Dtr
includes the candidate's own money or property used
on behal,f of his or her candidacy....
Regulation 18215(a) (2) (A), coPY
enclosed,
Thus, if the payment of scholarship funds is refated to your
candidacy or your stltus as an office holder, the payment is a
."p"it"Ui" 
"oirt.ibution subject to the limits imposed by Proposi-
tion 73. (Sections 85301 and 85303.)
Upon receipt of the scholarship funds, if you determine that
the palrnent is either a gift or incone, you nay deposit these
fund! lnto your campaign bank account. Nothi-nq in the Act
prevents yoir fro:o dlpositing your own personal funds into the
iarnpaign Lank account. (section 85201(d).) uoreover, although
the- Act places certain linits 
on contributions, there are no
linits 
on the anount of personal funds that a candidate nay
deposit into his canpaign account. (Section 85301(b).)
If you determine that the funds are a contribution, you must
first deierrnine whether the pa)mrent complies with the contribution
lirnits of Sections 85301 and 85303. If the funds exceed the
contribution linits they must be returned to the donor, prj-or to
deposit or negotiation, within the earliest of the following
deadlines:
(1) 10 working days of receiPt; or


Fi■e No. 90-459
Page 5
(2)  By the reporting dead■ine for the report―
ing period in which the contribution 
■s
received....
Regu■ation ■853■(b)(■)and (2)′  copy
enc■osed.
If the funds dO not exceed the contribution 
■im■ts′ the funds must
be depos■ted in your campaign bank account and reported as a
contribution to you。  (SeCtion 3520■(c)。
We trust this 
■etter adequate■y responds to your inqu■ry.
Shou■d you have any further questions regarding this matter′
 do
not hesitate to ca■■ me at (9■6)322-590■
.
Sincere■y′
Scott Ha■■abr■n
Acting cenera■ Counse■

counsel, Legal Division
